topics.title: Babe Ruth Biography and Tipping in Las Vegas
topics.summary: The studio discusses a new biography of Babe Ruth, its historical claims regarding his nicknames, and a listener's complaint about being told the host is cheap at tipping in Las Vegas.

topics.title: The Sopranos and Pop Culture
topics.summary: The studio discusses a variety of topics including the effectiveness of learning through music, listener feedback regarding Sirius satellite radio, a humorous clip of a preacher's sermon on social issues, and an analysis of recent plotlines and performances in The Sopranos.

topics.title: Pop Culture and Music Licensing
topics.summary: The studio discusses recent episodes of The Sopranos, celebrity sightings at sporting events, and the complexities of securing music rights for film and television.

topics.title: Media Dynamics and Celebrity Reputation
topics.summary: The studio discusses the influence of gossip columns like Page Six on celebrity reputations, the complexities of public relations during crises, and the impact of music selection in film.

topics.title: Media Dynamics and Industry Rivalries
topics.summary: The studio explores corporate influence at News Corp, the distinction between satellite radio audiences and traditional television ratings, and the transition of news anchors in major markets.

topics.title: Celebrity Gossip and Personal Anecdotes
topics.summary: The studio features a discussion on celebrity reputations, the personal lives of figures like Jennifer Aniston and Brad Pitt, and a guest's experiences with marriage, health issues, and the entertainment industry.

topics.title: Kathy Griffin Discusses Career and Personal Life
topics.summary: The show features Kathy Griffin discussing her reality series, her experiences performing for troops in Iraq, being banned from various talk shows, and her interactions with other celebrities.

topics.title: Kathy Griffin and Neighborhood Demographics
topics.summary: The show features Kathy Griffin discussing her upcoming television series before transitioning into a discussion about demographic shifts in New Jersey suburbs.

topics.title: Jewish Cultural Identity and Musical Tributes
topics.summary: The studio explores the cultural nuances of Jewish identity, the experience of attending a high-quality The Who tribute band, and various personal anecdotes regarding workplace dynamics and hygiene habits.

topics.title: Eric the Midget's Parental Concerns
topics.summary: The studio discusses Eric the Midget's concerns regarding his parents' reaction to his online presence and his refusal to participate in various stunts for a potential meeting with Catherine McPhee.

topics.title: Celebrity Encounters and Pop Culture Commentary
topics.summary: The studio explores a guest's interactions with celebrities, his experiences as a public figure, and various pop culture topics including film critiques and celebrity rumors.

topics.title: Satellite Radio Trends and Newsroom Dynamics
topics.summary: The studio discusses the growth of satellite radio driven by Howard Stern, the complexities of reporting on local news stories, and internal dynamics within the production team.

topics.title: Katie Court's Farewell to the Morning Show
topics.summary: The studio reacts to and mocks the overly sentimental farewell speech of co-anchor Katie Court as she departs from her long-running morning show position.

topics.title: Broadway Theater and Comedy Roast
topics.summary: The studio discusses Broadway theater performances, a comedy roast event featuring James Brown, and personal anecdotes regarding alcohol consumption.

topics.title: Musical Preparation and Fishing Anecdotes
topics.summary: The studio discusses plans for a musical performance of "Hot Pants," shares stories about fishing in rowboats, and reacts to news regarding O.J. Simpson and Marie Osmond's daughters.

topics.title: MySpace Controversy and Celebrity News
topics.summary: The show covers a variety of topics including a mother's reaction to her daughter's controversial MySpace content, updates on Donald Trump's family life, news regarding Whoopi Goldberg's upcoming radio show, and reports on various local crimes and tragic accidents.

topics.title: Entertainment News and Celebrity Updates
topics.summary: The studio covers a variety of topics including a listener's kidnapping story, the launch of a new music channel, box office results for X-Men and The Breakup, upcoming interviews with Heather Mills, and various comedy show announcements.

transcript.text: Good morning, everybody. What's happening?
And welcome to a live morning show right here at Howard 100. Howard 101.
We're back. We had a week off. Very nice. Very nice. Very nice weather. First couple of days we were off.
Beautiful Memorial Day weekend.
Yeah, boy, it was Memorial Day night.
Very nice. It was almost Caribbean-like here in New York.
Yeah.
Around 80-something degrees. Something like that. Very nice.
Yeah, it was summer. It wasn't playing around. It was summer.
Yeah. It was summer and I woke up and no Fred.
Unfortunate for you.
It was different. Let's put it that way. You know, I'm used to having Fred in my morning.
You usually look over and see Fred.
Yeah, and you could have called me up. I climbed into bed with you.
For a whole week I didn't see Fred.
That would have been interesting.
But good to see all of you.
Good to see you. How have you been?
I've been good. I got a book. I read a book over vacation. Did you know that Dana gave me a book to read?
I think so, yeah.
Dana has been telling everybody about that book.
It's a terrific book.
Oh, wait a minute. Is it about dogs?
Is it the one...
About an Afghanistan e-boy.
Oh, yeah, yeah. I've heard it.
The kite runner.
The kite runner. I read it.
You're trying to get me to read it.
I'm almost finished. I was devastated reading this book.
Was it good?
Fabulous.
Oh, yeah?
I wrote her an email.
You read it in one week.
I read it in one day.
In one day?
Yeah. It was so good.
How long of a book is it?
And Beth was like, "I can't stand when you read books."
Does it happen often?
You become obsessed with the book.
That's why I don't read books, because I've got to finish it.
If it's a good one, yeah.
It was very, very good.
What's it about? It's like...
It's about a boy growing up in Afghanistan, his relationship with his father.
But it was really interesting, because here we went and kicked a Taliban out of Afghanistan,
and we don't know much about the country. I mean, what do you really know about it?
It gives you a good...
Sort of a background of what Afghanistan is all about.
Yeah.
But it does just look like a nothing wasteland from the...
When we look at it on the news, it's bombing it.
But it was also like a relationship between a boy and his father and his son and his...
You know what I mean?
It was a whole... It was a family.
It was an interesting family story.
Terrifically written.
Simple to read.
No pictures. That's the only criticism I have.
Wasn't one drawing in a book?
No. One... I wait for one...
And I go, "Ooh, page of pictures. I don't have to read."
Pictures are fun in a book.
They are.
But this was fiction, so there weren't any pictures to be had.
Yeah. If I wrote fiction, I'd put pictures in. I don't care.
Do you think... When I'm reading a book like that, and it's a guy from Afghanistan,
who's a doctor who writes, I say to myself...
What kind of doctor could he be?
No. This is his story. This is no fiction.
Yeah, right. Yeah, exactly.
Oh, yeah, I said that too. I mean, come on.
My favorite book I ever read is still the first one I ever read.
The Hank Aaron story. 70 pages and then all pictures.
Well, I was reading The New York Times. There's a new Babe Ruth book.
I have it.
Yes, I was reading about that too.
Yeah, it's terrific.
Everything about Babe Ruth sort of doesn't show the...
You know, it just shows the romantic part of him.
Even the movies they made about him. It doesn't show...
Like, obviously, he had a dark side, this guy.
I mean, his first wife, he just abandoned in a Massachusetts home
to come party in New York.
He died at 52.
He died very young.
Because he drank in and eaten too much.
Every story, you see, is just like the jolly side.
I think this focus is more like a little bit on the dark side.
Right, and tells you what he was really like.
Right, exactly.
You know, I read the book. I just finished the book.
Oh, yeah?
It's a great book. I think it's called Big Bam or something like that.
Big Bam. Is that the book that they reveal that his nickname is "Nakerwood"?
Yes, that's right.
That's the book.
That's the book.
Why did you take Umbridge with that clip? Do we still have that clip?
Richard, if you could put it back up, I just took it down a minute ago.
Why do you take Umbridge with it?
Because in the book, they claim that Babe Ruth's nickname was "Nigger Lips".
Yes, and then it got shortened.
Yeah, to "Nig".
Which is just affectionate.
Hey, Nick.
Hey, Nick. What's up, buddy?
Well, it got shortened to "Nigger" and then "Nig".
Yeah, it just kept going. And then Nick became in.
Yeah, I got the clip if you want.
Go ahead and play it.
Nick's nickname from the start was "Nigger Lips".
He would hear the word "Nigger" infinitely more times in his childhood than Hank Aaron or Barry Bonds,
or any African-American slugger who chased his records ever did.
He would hear it more than Jackie Robinson did.
The word was his name, often contracted from "Nigger Lips" to "Nigger" to "Nig".
Any of the permutations applied.
That's the part I don't... If this is supposed to be a researched biography of the great Bambino,
how would they know that he was called "Nigger Lips" more than for "Nigger"?
Yeah, how do they back up this claim?
Yeah, that's one of those statements. There's no way you could say that.
But anyway, we brought that up the other day.
Well, a lot of people think he was part black Babe Ruth.
Ty Cobb being one of them. Ty Cobb the famous racist.
Now, where did he base that up?
His looks.
Well, he had a sort of background that was mysterious, and he would base it on his looks.
Ty Cobb was quoted at an all-star game that was supposed to room together.
And Ty Cobb in the paper said, "I never bet it down with a nigger before. I'm not going to do it now."
And they asked him what he... This is one of the greatest players in the game, Zane.
They printed out the paper, just like that?
It was another day and time.
1918 or whatever. It was okay.
Boy, you know, when we named Gary Baba Bowie, I never thought to call him a horrible racist man.
Because Babe Ruth had very full, big lips, evidently.
Although I was looking at pictures of Babe Ruth, I didn't think he had...
They said it was olive skin, he had a broad nose and full lips. That was the thing.
And they said that he looked to be of mixed race.
Oh, that sounds like an Arab to me, not a black Arab.
Lisa, you're on the air. Good morning. Lisa's calling us from Las Vegas, so it must be urgent.
Okay, let's see. Why are we not hearing Lisa? Phones are not up.
Hi, Lisa, you're going to have to start all over again because your phone was...
Scott didn't press my phone button. That's your job.
Scott, okay. I want to know why you never take any negative phone calls anymore.
Why I don't take... I take all phone calls. I don't... Maybe because people like us here. I don't know.
Maybe there are none.
Fuck you!
I read my negative email, but I...
I wish that was the case, Howard, but just a couple days ago, I was at a party.
Okay.
And I was telling them how great you are and how much I love Sirius.
And they tore me to shreds. Not only all the usual stuff people say about, "He's a womanizer," blah, blah, blah.
And I'm trying to come to your defense, okay? But...
Why bother?
But here's the thing that really bothered me.
They told me that when you were in Vegas, you were really cheap.
And that you stiffed a lot of people.
Oh! Where'd that happen?
I'm going to tell you the truth. I didn't know what to say.
Well, how would they know... Excuse me, Lisa. Excuse me.
What a dumb comment. How would they know what I did in Vegas vis-a-vis tipping people?
Because these people apparently served you, waited on you...
Well, that's ridiculous.
Where?
When you came into Vegas this last time. There were some people that weren't there.
I don't know. I must have dropped about six grand the last day just tipping the entire staff.
This is why I called and I'm so glad I got through because I want you to say that because I didn't know what to say.
And let me just say this. When you live in Las Vegas, this is a tipping town.
I mean, people here, the dealers, the cocktail waitresses, the servers, they make minimum wage.
A lot of people don't know that.
I'm bored out of my skull, are you?
I'm surprised someone invited her to a party.
God help us, Jesus. God and baby, Jesus help us.
What was the insult already? I was talking. I didn't hear you.
No, you're incredibly boring is what we said.
Oh, well maybe you're incredibly fucking cheap. And I struck a guy with a zappi.
No, you're just incredibly boring. You're talking about Las Vegas being a tipping town. I mean, who cares?
Other people have called to insult us in a real entertaining way.
A million dollars.
Five hundred million dollars. Five hundred million dollars.
It sounds like you're, I think you project everything onto other people.
It sounds like you count everyone's money.
That's the story that happened. And I love you, Howard. I've listened to you.
Oh, you fucking annoying cock.
You stupid cocksucker.
Oh my God. I fucking hate her.
Is there a way to take her off the serious subscription?
Take away her subscription.
What people don't know is Las Vegas is a tipping town.
Maybe people, maybe I had a nerve because you're incredibly fucking cheap.
And there's no negative calls.
I don't understand. Even that, she didn't care about whether there are negative calls or not.
She had a story about a party she went to in Vegas.
There are people who think even if you lose money, you're supposed to tip the dealer.
I am not one of them. If I win five G's at the table, I'll throw the guy a couple hundred.
If I lose it, he ain't getting shit.
You want to know something? I'm so glad to hear you say that because I get such pressure when I go to these tables in Vegas.
I don't even go anymore because I hate it so much because everybody's standing there waiting for tips.
And I'm losing my shirt. And it's like, no, if I'm winning, I'll give you some money.
But I have to start tipping people even when I'm losing.
I over tip. I over tip when I win.
See, this is why I don't gamble.
I get so crazy and I'm drunk and I'm winning all over tip. But losing, it's like, fuck off, man.
How about a free steak somewhere around here?
I got to worry more about what other people are thinking. You know what I mean?
Let me ask you something, though. Was there a story once written that you were a bad tipper in Vegas?
Because that's probably what...
Set it off?
Yeah.
I don't know. Anyone who knows me knows I tip way too much.
In fact, it's obnoxious. I get my American Express bill from these restaurants I go to.
And I usually give at least a 50% tip.
No, I've seen it. I've witnessed it firsthand.
I took my parents out to dinner Saturday night. And it was like a $600 bill.
And I left $300 for the waiter. I mean, I don't know. Is that cheap?
Yes.
I mean, give me a break. I don't know.
It must be. People are saying you're cheap.
What am I supposed to do? I don't know.
In years, you have always done this. And then that one story, I think, is fueling this.
Because some guy on the Internet claimed I didn't tip him.
And it was one of those websites that...
And wasn't he like a dealer or something when you dropped a couple hundred bucks or something?
Yes, sorry.
There's people in Vegas who you don't even realize you have to tip sometimes.
I think their job is to be tipped.
Well, you know what it is. They see Howard Stern sitting there.
And they go, "Oh, he should be giving me money."
Even while you're losing. They don't care about how much you're losing.
I'm sitting there trying to play.
My point is, when I'm losing, I'm giving your boss money. Get it from him.
Work out a better deal.
Yeah, I gave it to the office.
This is a tipping town, you know what? So maybe you ought to get a union together
and stop with all the tipping and get real wage.
It seems that those hotels are making a lot of money.
Yeah, that's why they're making a lot of money.
They're depending on us to pay everyone.
Vegas is like a friend who won money off you and constantly shows off his jewelry he bought with it.
You walk into Vegas and they built the fucking Eiffel Tower.
I'm like, "This is how much of a loser your people are. We built the Brooklyn Bridge here."
Well, now they've made it so you don't have to travel. Just come there. You can go anywhere.
Yeah, we'd feel a lot better if you didn't have the Eiffel Tower in your backyard.
I mean, Jesus, give me a break. I can look like you're at least hurting a little.
I heard a lot of the show while I was on vacation because whenever I'd be driving around in my car,
I'd put it on. It's so cool to put on the show whenever you want it.
I know.
It is cool, these channels.
It's like having your friend there whenever you need it.
No, I had a good time listening. I found it was a crack. I just had to keep tuning in.
Do you find you're critiquing yourself?
Yeah, sometimes. Sometimes I'm a little rough on myself.
Sometimes I wish I'd let something go longer or sometimes I wish I'd shortened something up.
But on the whole, I'm pretty fucking great.
No, I do. I'm hard on myself.
There was something I was listening to and I was like, "Boy, we let this go on way too long."
This was way too involved. It was Andrea, as a matter of fact.
In fact, I was watching it on...
Howard TV?
Yeah. And I was like, "You know, there's a lot of things I could have done."
What do you mean? Her first or her second half?
It was the first half where we couldn't really get anything out of her.
Yeah, that was bad. But then she went and had a couple of drinks, came back.
Why did she get good?
She was great. Everybody wanted...
You know, it was funny. That first interview we did with her where I'm trying to get something out of her.
Everyone's riding in, get rid of her. She can't be Miss Howard Stern anymore.
She's horrible on the air. You got to get rid of her.
She comes back after a few drinks, starts singing.
As a matter of fact, that was one of my criticisms.
I love the singing.
I cut off the singing too fast.
Yeah, the singing was great.
We should play that right now. That was great. That first song she does.
You got that, Fred?
I'll take a look.
Miss Howard Stern.
We should just get her drunk and have her do karaoke.
And then everyone was riding me. You got to keep her as Miss Howard Stern. She's the greatest.
After that.
So, you know, you never know. Sometimes you just keep trying to chase the gold.
You know, she's got it in her. She's just got to come out with it.
But...
I was in a hotel this weekend down outside of Miami and I saw the fucking machines episode.
It was in the hotel.
Oh, your dedication.
I was looking by. I was going to watch a movie and I saw that.
And I said, let me see what episodes they have available.
And everybody who I know hasn't talked about the fucking machines.
I said, let me watch this. Holy shit. Man, that's television.
That's like Norman Lear had no idea what he was doing.
But when Gary gets hit, it's almost like watching the Zapruder film.
You're anticipating when it's going to come and his head goes back into the left like Kennedy's.
And it's really fascinating.
Donny, you're on the air.
Hey, Howard. How's it going? You know, I hate to be another caller with bad news.
But I just don't get how you could sit here and have this show.
We're paying $13 a month and you take a vacation every single week.
Well, first of all, I was on Howard Stern, what is it called? Stern Fan Network?
They're like, I'm sick and tired of Howard taking so much vacation.
I'm going, those motherfuckers, six months we've been here.
I think we've taken two weeks.
We've actually taken less vacation than we used to.
We canceled a bunch of vacations.
Let's look at the facts there. Every single week we take a vacation. Is that your point?
Since we've been here, every single week we take vacations.
As soon as you go away, then you've been away forever.
That's the problem with the easiest hundred million I've ever seen in my life.
Five hundred million? Oh, five hundred million.
And you have to sit in your chair and talk all day. God damn, that's a hard job.
Boy, I tell you, a lot of things, a lot of jealousy coming up.
You never pretended that this was splitting the atom.
I mean, hey, sorry. Why don't you go get a job on radio if you think it's so great?
You hooked me up, brother.
Nobody hooked me up.
See, that's what it comes down to.
Alright, sorry you're sick of our vacation, but what can I do?
By the way, this week, we've got a big week.
Oh, it's the hearty roast week, isn't it?
Yeah, as a matter of fact, when I was listening, I listened back to the Ralph roast.
It was good that I listened back to what I learned a few things about my role during the roast and what I need to do.
I thought it was really good listening to it, but this week, we're going to try something different with our schedule.
Monday through Thursday, of course, we'll do the show here in the morning.
And then Thursday evening, around six o'clock, we're going to do another show.
We're going to do two shows on Thursday.
Thursday evening, we're going to do Artie's roast so we can drink and stuff.
And that will be broadcast live, right?
That will be broadcast live, but then it will be re-aired Friday morning.
So you can either choose to listen to it in the morning or at night.
Let's see.
What time is the roast, do you know?
I think six, I want to say.
Six o'clock.
Yeah, six o'clock.
Be here or not be here.
Let me see if I can get this thing going.
A little rusty here.
That's not the one I like.
I guess the way that it was.
Can't even catch a good bus.
'Cause I'd be worried about who's driving home, who's got the keys, who's got the phone.
Who paid the cab?
Who paid the cab?
Who didn't pay too bad?
But I don't want that responsibility.
And I can't be with a woman, baby.
This drunk or the mean.
Why'd I come to this when I say I have another?
Baby, I heard it in me and I became my mother, well.
But I'm thinking, you're interfering with my drinking.
I'd have been through to a present situation.
You and me out on the town got built-in limitations, whoa.
Let me make this clear.
So I'm the one who's drinking here.
I'm not worried about who's driving home, who's got the keys, who's got the phone.
Who'll pay the bill?
Call the cab.
Don't mean to make you mad, but I don't want that responsibility.
I can't be with a woman, baby.
I'm in, who gets drunk or the mean?
America's drunk.
Yay!
A couple of things about that.
She was drinking Jack Daniels all morning.
And you should see, she takes that bottle.
That was one of the things about watching it.
Because you get to see her back in the studio with Sal and Richard.
And she just takes that bottle and upends it and doesn't stop.
That's on Howard TV, huh?
Yeah.
Drinking JD like Gleason.
Oh, my goodness.
Yeah, that's what it got good.
And, you know, I was thinking about this.
You know, she's a girl who, we assume, doesn't have all that much intelligence.
But here she could learn that entire song effortlessly.
And, you know, I was thinking about this.
Even when my kids went to school, whenever there was a rough topic,
they would teach them a song about it.
And the kids, like learning through music, there's something about it.
Because, like, if you gave her those lyrics on a sheet of paper
and told her to memorize them...
Yeah, they were just written down.
Yeah, if she was in a movie and they asked her to memorize her part,
she would have the most difficult time.
But look at that, that song, boom, she got it all down.
I used to tell my teachers, like, if they put the Pythagorean Theorem
in an episode of The Odd Couple, I'd know it by heart.
Yeah.
Right, it doesn't make any sense.
But songs, you think about how many songs you know by heart
or just a little bit by heart, you know.
Yeah, so what are you going to do?
She loves that one.
Hey, Mary, you're on the air.
And that's a tough one.
Mary lives in Manhattan.
Hi, Mary.
Oh, hi, Howard.
I just wanted to tell you that I think you're a better tipper than Paul McCartney.
Not that I've ever waited on you, but I did wait on Paul.
And from what you just said about how you tip, you tip better than him.
Oh, I'm a real good tipper.
He's a bazillionaire, so I just wanted to prop you up with that.
That's okay, I can take the criticism.
We don't have to hit on Paul, you know.
No, no, he's a good tipper, too.
I'm not saying he's bad, but you're better.
Right, thank you.
People don't base it on the tip.
They base it on how much you make.
Yeah.
Like what she just said there, he's a bazillionaire.
So he should tip more than probably 40%.
Right, if you're tipping 50, I guess he should give you the whole bill as a tip, too.
Hey, Sandy, you're on the air in York, Pennsylvania.
What's happening?
Wow, Howard.
Hey, I just wanted to let you know, you know, there may be a lot of listeners
that came over from your old show, but I happen to be a new listener.
And you know what?
He's never got you before.
And then I watched you on Larry King.
Yes.
And you explain what you're about, and I got you, and I love it.
And I got to tell you, I cannot get enough of the crazy Alice stuff.
Yeah, that's good.
So funny.
And if you can dig it back up, do you remember the preacher that you had on early on?
It had to be like the first week or two.
That stuff was hysterical.
You haven't played it for a while, but that was really funny.
Yeah, I know exactly what you're talking about.
Let me write that down.
That's good.
All right.
Do you want to hear the preacher?
I'll listen to that again.
And we do want to do a tribute to Dan, the song parody man, and some of the crazy Alice stuff.
Yeah, we never got around to that last time.
Yeah, all right.
Okay.
You know, it's funny.
My husband used to listen to you all the time, and I never got you.
And then I watched Larry King, and I was about to buy an XM radio, and I said,
"No, I'm getting serious."
Oh, good.
I got him one for Father's Day, and we both love you.
Thank you very much.
As a matter of fact, there's got some good press over the week that we were gone.
So one article about how Sirius now is surpassing XM in terms of -- well, in a lot of terms, actually.
In terms of recognition factor, it used to be XM was the only satellite people knew,
and Sirius has surpassed them, and they did studies about how the Howard Stern Show has made the difference in Sirius.
People are now aware of it and enjoying it and using it and subscribing to it.
So, I mean, things couldn't be better.
Jeez, I ought to -- without some of that stuff, I just hope it's interesting to listen to.
You know what?
I heard something the other day.
Martha Stewart is going on QVC.
Oh, yeah.
And I guess she's selling Sirius.
Or maybe they're simulcasting.
I don't know.
There was something about Sirius in the Martha Stewart QVC announcement.
I'd like to see a few of the higher-profile people around here get off their ass and start selling some Sirius.
Yeah, that's the first time I've seen somebody else selling it.
Yeah.
I'm going to invade her segment.
I'm going to walk on her QVC segment.
No, she should.
I mean, she's Martha Stewart.
Women love her ass, and she's got a whole channel over there.
Get out there and promote.
Absolutely.
I mean, it's the future of this company.
All right, so I'll -- you know the preacher she's talking about?
Fred, remember that preacher we played?
I got the song.
I know he's from Washington, D.C., or something.
What did he say?
It wasn't the first week we were on.
It's the one where it's causing problems in families.
Right, right, right.
Homosexuality.
Women.
Preview page two, it's a purple.
It's probably like from March or something.
What, page two?
It's a Gary preview page two.
Is this a guy whose son couldn't get a date to the prom?
Is that the one?
Yeah.
Yeah.
People want to hear that.
If you can't find it, I have it.
I got it.
Gary preview page two in what, in white?
In purple.
Purple.
My favorite's the NBC jingle page.
Yeah, yeah.
I'll get to that page one time.
You still could make it.
And we're living a time now.
Brothers have been so put down, can't get a job.
A lot of the sisters making more money than brothers
and it's created problems in families.
That's one of the reasons our family's breaking up,
